title: implement bs cmux add
description:
Implement bs cmux add --cwd <path> [--name <name>] [--agent <agent>] command in the bs CLI, analogous to bs tmux add.

What needs to be done:
- Add cli/cmd/cmux/ package with cmux.go (parent command), workspace.go (add subcommand)
- Add cli/internal/cmux/ package with Add() function and Executor interface
- Wire into cli/cmd/root.go

Layout to create:
  Left pane  : agent command staged, no Enter — claude staged as 'claude agents --cwd <cwd>'
  Top-right  : ls -al && bd ready (executed)
  Bottom-right: lazygit (executed)

Implementation approach:
  1. cmux new-workspace (creates workspace, captures CMUX_WORKSPACE_ID if available)
  2. cmux new-split right (creates right column; active surface is now right)
  3. cmux send "ls -al && bd ready" then cmux send-key enter (top-right)
  4. cmux new-split down (splits right column; active surface is now bottom-right)
  5. cmux send "lazygit" then cmux send-key enter
  6. Focus left pane via cmux list-surfaces --json + cmux focus-surface, then cmux send "claude agents --cwd <cwd>" (no enter)

CWD must be passed to all panes; cmux does not have a per-split -c flag like tmux, so cd into the directory as the first command in each pane, or rely on the shell's inherited CWD if the workspace opens in the right directory (needs testing).

Allowed agents: claude, codex, gemini, opencode, pi (same as tmux version). Only claude gets the 'agents --cwd <cwd>' expansion.

Acceptance criteria:
- bs cmux add --cwd ~/code/myproject opens a workspace with the 3-pane layout
- All panes are cwd'd to the provided path
- Claude is staged as 'claude agents --cwd <path>' without executing
- Other agents are staged as their bare name without executing
- --name overrides the workspace label if cmux supports naming
- Requires cmux to be installed; errors clearly if not found
close_reason: Implemented cli/cmd/cmux and cli/internal/cmux packages with bs cmux add command matching bs tmux add layout

title: bs cmux: workspace management commands
description: Add a bs cmux command group that provides cmux-based workspace management analogous to the existing bs tmux commands. cmux is a macOS-native terminal built on Ghostty for managing multiple AI coding agents. It exposes a socket-based CLI for workspace, split, surface, and status control. This epic covers implementing add, start, and reset subcommands plus personal cmux config tracking.

title: tmux session-closed hook: auto-clear claude-pending flag on session death
description:
## Why
Flag files in ~/.local/share/claude-pending/ persist when a tmux session is killed or the machine restarts. Without a cleanup hook, stale entries accumulate for sessions that no longer exist.

## What needs to be done
- Add a session-closed hook to tmux.conf that deletes the flag file for the dying session:
  set-hook -g session-closed 'run-shell "rm -f ~/.local/share/claude-pending/#{session_name}"'
- This handles kill-session, exit, and any other session teardown path
- Does not cover reboots (tmux doesn't fire hooks on shutdown) — stale reboot entries are handled by prune-on-open in the popup picker (bs-ad5)

## Acceptance criteria
- Killing a tmux session removes its flag file
- Surviving sessions are unaffected
- Pairs with bs-ad5 prune-on-open for full staleness coverage

## Parent epic
Part of bs-en9: Epic: tmux Claude pending notification system

title: Epic: tmux Claude pending notification system
description:
Tracks the full system for surfacing which tmux sessions have a Claude agent waiting for attention.

## Why
macOS notifications are ephemeral. When running multiple concurrent Claude sessions, there's no persistent way to know which sessions have stopped and need a response. This epic delivers two complementary UIs on top of a shared state layer.

## Deliverables
- State layer: flag files written by claude-notify, cleared on navigation
- Popup picker: fzf popup (prefix+N) with live pane preview for browsing pending sessions
- Status bar: second tmux status line showing pending sessions above the existing Dracula bar

title: claude-notify state layer: write and clear per-session flag files
description:
Shared foundation for the tmux pending-notification features. Extends claude-notify to persist session state to disk and clears it on navigation.

## Why
Both the popup picker and the two-status-line indicator need to know which sessions have pending Claude notifications. This state must survive across tmux sessions and be readable by shell scripts. A flag-file-per-session approach is simple, low-latency, and requires no daemon.

## What needs to be done

### Write flags (in claude-notify)
- On Stop or Notification hook fire, write a file to ~/.local/share/claude-pending/<session_name>
- File contents (one field per line or JSON): project name (cwd_project), tmux target (session:window.pane), timestamp (epoch seconds)
- Create the directory if it doesn't exist

### Clear flags (tmux hooks)
- Add an 'after-select-window' hook in tmux.conf that deletes the flag file for the current session when you switch to it
- This ensures the indicator clears automatically when you actually visit the session

### Helpers (optional but useful)
- A small helper function or script that lists pending sessions (reads all flag files, emits formatted output) — shared by both consumers rather than duplicated

## Acceptance criteria
- claude-notify writes a flag file when Stop/Notification fires
- Flag file is deleted when the user switches to that session's window
- Flag directory is created automatically if missing
- Works correctly with multiple concurrent Claude sessions
acceptance_criteria: flag written on hook fire; flag cleared on window switch; multi-session works correctly
close_reason: flag write/clear and helper script implemented and pushed

title: Two tmux status lines: pending notifications on top, Dracula on bottom
description:
Add a second tmux status line so pending Claude sessions are always visible without displacing the existing Dracula status bar.

## Why
The current single-line Dracula status bar has no room for a persistent pending-session indicator without fighting Dracula's ownership of status-right. A second status line sidesteps that entirely and gives full layout control.

## Layout preference
- Status line 1 (top row): pending Claude sessions — blank/invisible when nothing is waiting, shows session names with a visual indicator (e.g. ⧖) when sessions need attention
- Status line 2 (bottom row): existing Dracula output, completely untouched

This order keeps Dracula at the bottom where it currently lives (muscle memory preserved) and adds the notification layer above it.

## What needs to be done

### State layer (shared with popup bead)
- Extend ~/.local/bin/claude-notify to write flag files under ~/.local/share/claude-pending/ (same state layer as the popup feature — both features share this)
- Clear flags on session/window navigation via tmux hooks

### Status line configuration
- Set 'set -g status 2' in dotfiles/.config/tmux/tmux.conf (requires tmux 3.2+, verify first)
- status-format[1] = top row: a #() segment that reads pending flag files and emits session names, or empty string when none pending
- status-format[0] = bottom row: leave as Dracula's output — do not manually set, let Dracula own it
- Style the top row: muted/invisible background when empty (zero visual noise); highlighted (e.g. amber) when sessions are pending

## Visual behavior
Nothing pending — top row is blank, effectively invisible:
  bootstrap  1:zsh  2:nvim  3:claude       ✓ main  ⚡ 42%  CPU 12%  RAM 4.2G  14:32

Sessions pending — top row appears above Dracula:
  ⧖ api-service · bootstrap · infra
  bootstrap  1:zsh  2:nvim  3:claude       ✓ main  ⚡ 42%  CPU 12%  RAM 4.2G  14:32

## Acceptance criteria
- 'set -g status 2' confirmed working on installed tmux version
- Top row invisible when no pending sessions
- Top row shows session names when Claude has stopped and is waiting
- Bottom row (Dracula) unchanged in appearance and behavior
- Pending state clears when session is visited
acceptance_criteria: top row invisible when idle; shows pending session names when waiting; Dracula bottom row unchanged; clears on navigation

title: tmux popup for browsing pending Claude notifications
description:
Add a tmux keybinding (prefix + N) that opens a display-popup with an fzf list of sessions where Claude has stopped and is awaiting attention.

## Why
macOS notifications are ephemeral — they disappear before you get to them, leaving no way to see which sessions still need a response. This popup provides a persistent, navigable queue.

## What needs to be done

### Popup script (~/.local/bin/claude-pending-picker)
- On open: prune stale flag files whose session name doesn't appear in `tmux ls` — handles reboot orphans silently
- Read remaining flag files and format for fzf: project name, tmux target, elapsed time (e.g. '14 min ago')
- fzf preview pane: show last N lines of that pane's scrollback via `tmux capture-pane -t <target> -p -e`
- On selection (Enter): switch to the session (`tmux switch-client -t <target>`) and delete its flag file
- Keybind d (dismiss selected): delete the flag file for the focused entry without navigating; reload list
- Keybind D (dismiss all): delete all flag files and close popup
- On abort (Esc/q): dismiss without side effects
- Empty state: show 'no pending sessions' message when nothing is waiting

### tmux integration
- Bind prefix + N to `display-popup -E -w 80% -h 60% ~/.local/bin/claude-pending-picker`
- Add to dotfiles/.config/tmux/tmux.conf

## Acceptance criteria
- Pressing prefix+N shows all pending sessions with elapsed time
- Moving cursor in fzf shows live pane preview for that session
- Selecting an entry navigates directly to that session and removes it from the list
- Pressing d dismisses the focused entry (removes flag) without navigating
- Pressing D clears all pending flags and closes the popup
- Pressing Esc dismisses without side effects
- Stale entries from reboots are pruned automatically on popup open
- Empty state handled gracefully
acceptance_criteria: prefix+N opens popup; fzf preview shows pane scrollback on cursor move; selection navigates and clears flag; empty state handled gracefully

title: track personal cmux config changes
description:
Capture and version-control personal cmux configuration as changes are made to the setup.

cmux reads from:
  - ~/.config/ghostty/config  (terminal rendering — already tracked via dotfiles symlink)
  - ~/.config/cmux/cmux.json  (app settings — needs symlink)
  - .cmux/cmux.json            (project-scoped — per-repo, not tracked here)

What needs to be done:
- Add dotfiles/.config/cmux/cmux.json with personal preferences as they evolve
- Wire it into scripts/install.sh as a symlink (or verify make install covers ~/.config/ files automatically)
- Document notable config sections used: app, terminal, notifications, sidebar, automation, shortcuts

This is an ongoing / living bead — update it as new cmux config preferences are discovered and committed. Not a one-time implementation task.

Acceptance criteria:
- ~/.config/cmux/cmux.json is a symlink into the dotfiles repo
- Personal cmux preferences are committed and survive a fresh machine bootstrap
close_reason: cmux.json captured from plist, symlinked via make install, pushed

title: implement bs cmux reset
description:
Implement bs cmux reset command analogous to bs tmux reset.

Depends on bs cmux start being implemented first.

Proposed behavior:
  bs cmux reset
  - Closes all open cmux workspaces (list-workspaces --json + close-workspace --workspace <id> for each)
  - Then calls the same workspace-recreation logic as bs cmux start (no restore, clean slate)
  - Must be run from outside cmux or handle graceful self-close

Research questions:
1. Does cmux have a way to close all workspaces in one command, or does it require iterating?
2. What happens when the calling terminal is itself inside a cmux workspace that gets closed?
3. Is there a cmux equivalent to tmux kill-server?

Acceptance criteria (draft):
- bs cmux reset closes all workspaces and rebuilds from config
- Errors clearly if no workspaces are defined
- Handles the 'running inside cmux' case (warn or refuse, like tmux version)

title: implement bs cmux start
description:
Implement bs cmux start command analogous to bs tmux start. This ticket requires research before implementation.

Research questions:
1. Does cmux have a native workspace config format (beyond .cmux/cmux.json) that defines workspace layouts declaratively?
2. Can cmux restore-session recreate workspace splits and surface commands, or only layout+dirs?
3. Is there a cmux analog to tmuxinator YAML for scripting workspace creation from config files?
4. How does cmux handle workspaces that already exist (idempotency)?

Proposed behavior (to refine after research):
  bs cmux start [--no-restore] [--override]
  - Reads workspace config files from ~/.config/cmux/workspaces/*.json (or YAML — TBD)
  - Optionally runs cmux restore-session before creating defined workspaces
  - --override: close existing workspace with same name before recreating
  - --no-restore: skip the restore-session step

Config format TBD based on research — either reuse the tmuxinator YAML schema or define a cmux-native JSON schema.

Acceptance criteria (draft):
- bs cmux start creates workspaces defined in config files
- Existing workspaces are left untouched unless --override is passed
- Restore step can be skipped with --no-restore
- Config format is documented
close_reason: implemented bs cmux start with --no-restore, --override, JSON workspace configs, local overlay dir, and restore-session integration
